title: Huldra's Tail
summary: A skilled hunter falls for a beautiful Huldra, accepting her secret animal tail in a rare display of unconditional love that challenges the nature of forest spirits.
full_text:
Deep in the forests of Hälsingland, where the pines grow so dense that the sun becomes a rumor and the moss hangs from the branches like old men's beards, there lived a hunter named Björn. He was known throughout the parish for his skill with a bow and his knowledge of the woods. He could track a deer across bare rock and read the forest like a book. But he knew little of the world beyond the trees, and nothing at all of the creatures that shared those woods with him.

One autumn evening, as the leaves turned to copper and gold, Björn was tracking a magnificent stag — a beast with antlers like the branches of an ancient oak. The stag led him deeper than he had ever gone, to a clearing where a stream ran over stones smooth as glass. And there, kneeling at the water's edge, was a woman.

She was the most beautiful woman Björn had ever seen. Her hair was the color of birch bark in sunlight, long and flowing past her waist. Her skin was pale as milk, her eyes green as young leaves. She wore a dress woven from moss and lichen, and on her head rested a crown of wildflowers. When she turned and smiled at him, Björn forgot the stag, forgot the forest, forgot his own name.

He stepped closer. And then he saw it — a bushy cow's tail peeking from beneath her skirt, twitching like an animal with a life of its own.

He knew then what she was: a huldra, a forest spirit. The old tales said she could grant luck or bring ruin. But Björn did not flee. He knelt before her. "I have never seen such beauty," he said.

The huldra laughed — a sound like water running over stones. "You see my tail. Why do you not run?"

"Because I love what I see," said Björn. "Tail and all."

They courted through the autumn. Björn left offerings of bread and honey at the edge of the clearing. The huldra taught him to read the language of birds and find the hidden springs. When winter came, she agreed to marry him, on one condition: he must never speak of her tail to anyone. Not to his mother, not to the priest, not even in jest. If he did, she would vanish forever.

Björn agreed. They were married in the forest, with no priest but the moon, and the huldra came to live in his cottage. The first year was a miracle. The farm prospered as never before. The cow gave the richest milk. The crops grew tall. Björn's luck in hunting became legendary. The huldra bore him a son, and then a daughter, and his heart was full.

But the village was full of whispers. The other wives noticed that the strange woman never came to church. They noticed her odd clothes, her too-green eyes. At the midsummer feast, when the beer flowed freely, a drunkard clapped Björn on the shoulder and said, "I hear your wife has a tail under those fine skirts!"

The table went silent. Björn felt the blood rush to his face. "That's a lie!" he shouted, though he knew it was not.

The huldra, sitting at the far end of the table, heard everything. Her green eyes met Björn's. And in that look, he saw the end.

That night, she stood in the doorway of their cottage, her children clinging to her skirts. "You spoke of it," she said. "The spell is broken."

"I denied it!" Björn pleaded. "I protected you!"

"You denied me," she said. "You were ashamed. Shame is worse than betrayal." She stepped outside, and the children — who were not entirely human — followed her, their eyes glowing like small moons. "We go to the forest now. Do not follow."

Björn spent the rest of his life searching the woods. He grew old and gray, his bow arm forgotten, his farm neglected. Sometimes, in the deepest part of the forest, he would catch a glimpse of a woman with flowing hair and a crown of flowers, and his children — though they were grown now — running beside her. But whenever he called out, the forest would shift, and they would vanish.

The huldra never returned. But the hunters of Hälsingland still tell the tale: if you see a beautiful woman in the forest with a tail beneath her skirts, do not mock her. Bow politely, and say, "I see your petticoat shows, milady." And if she smiles, your hunt will be blessed. But if you shame her, she will take your luck and vanish into the trees, and you will search for her until your dying day.
moral: True acceptance means loving someone completely, including their hidden nature.

title: Hansel and Gretel
summary: Children abandoned in the forest discover a witch's gingerbread house and must use their wits to survive.
full_text:
Deep in the Black Forest, where the pines grow so thick they block the sun and the wind whispers secrets of old and forgotten things, there lived a poor woodcutter with his second wife. He had two children from his first marriage—Hansel, the elder brother, and Gretel, his sweet little sister. The stepmother hated them, secretly wishing they would disappear into the shadows of the pines. She convinced the woodcutter that there was not enough food for four people. "If we do not leave the children in the forest," she whispered, her voice a serpent's hiss, "we shall all starve together."

One morning, the father led the children deep into the forest, further than ever before. "Wait here," he said, his voice trembling, "while I fetch wood. Stay by the fire, and we shall return for you." But Hansel, a boy of keen ears and a sharper mind, had overheard the plan. He had spent the night gathering white pebbles from the garden, dropping them behind him like shimmering breadcrumbs. As the parents disappeared into the trees, the children waited until the moon rose, and by the light of the silver orb, Hansel followed the pebbles back home. The father wept with joy, but the stepmother's heart remained a frozen stone.

Months later, the famine returned, and the stepmother insisted on a second attempt. This time, the father locked the door at night, and Hansel could not gather pebbles. In desperation, he used a piece of hardened bread, crumbling it as they walked. But the birds of the forest—the crows and the jays—descended in a black cloud, devouring every crumb. The children were truly lost, wandering for days through a landscape of twisting roots and oppressive silence, until they encountered a bird with feathers of pure gold, who led them to a clearing.\n\nIn the center of the clearing stood a house that defied all reason. Its walls were made of gingerbread, frosting dripped from its windows like frozen waterfalls, and it smelled of cinnamon, sugar, and warm cloves. Driven by a hunger that had become a physical pain, Hansel and Gretel fell upon the house, tearing away pieces of the walls and licking the frosting from the eaves. As they feasted, the door creaked open, and an old woman emerged. She was hunched, her eyes clouded with cataracts, her voice a sugary croon that hid a predatory hunger. "Oh, you poor, lost children!" she exclaimed. "Come inside, and I shall feed you until you are plump and happy."

The hospitality was a facade. The woman was a witch—a child-eater who fattened her victims on sweets before roasting them in her oven. She locked Hansel in a small iron cage in the woodshed and forced Gretel to work as a slave, cooking great pots of stew and scrubbing floors. Every morning, the witch peered through the bars at Hansel and cursed, "Bones, not fat!" For she was too old and foolish to see that Hansel was holding out a small, dry bone instead of his finger, tricking her into believing he was still lean.

At last, the witch declared that tomorrow would be the day of the feast. Gretel, as the witch bent to check the oven's heat, realized it was a trap. "I do not know how to enter," she said innocently. "Could you show me how it is done?" The greedy witch, eager to finish her task, leaned forward and thrust her head into the oven. In a flash of courage, Gretel pushed her in and slammed the iron door shut. The witch's screams echoed through the cottage, but the children did not linger. They gathered the witch's hoard of diamonds and pearls, filled their pockets, and followed the golden bird back through the forest. When they finally reached their home, they found their stepmother gone and their father waiting for them with open arms, their poverty ended by the treasures they had brought back from the dark heart of the woods.
moral: Cunning and courage overcome even the darkest evil.

source_references.author: Brüder Grimm

source_references.title: Hansel and Gretel: The Brothers Grimm
source_references.author: Jack Zipes (trans.)

title: The Horned Women
summary: The night lay heavy over the Irish countryside, a winter's night so cold that the thatch on the cottage roofs crackled with frost and the breath of sleeping cattle rose in white clouds from the byres.
full_text: The night lay heavy over the Irish countryside, a winter's night so cold that the thatch on the cottage roofs crackled with frost and the breath of sleeping cattle rose in white clouds from the byres. In a stone house at the edge of a bog, a wealthy woman sat alone by the turf fire, carding wool into soft rolls for spinning. Her family and servants had gone to bed, and the only sounds were the crackling of the fire and the scrape of her carders against the wool. A knock came at the door. "Open! Open!" a voice called from outside. "Who is there?" the woman asked, setting down her carders. "I am the Witch of the One Horn." The woman hesitated. The voice was not familiar, but it was a bitter night, and no traveler should be left in the cold. She opened the door. A woman entered, wrapped in a grey cloak, carrying a pair of wool carders in her hand. On her forehead, growing like a goat's horn, was a single bony protrusion. She sat down by the fire without a word, took the wool, and began to card with furious speed. Before the mistress could speak, a second knock came. "Open! Open!" "Who is there?" "I am the Witch of the Two Horns." A second woman entered, with two horns on her forehead, carrying a spinning wheel. She sat beside the first and began to spin, her wheel whirring like a storm wind. The knocks continued, one after another, and each time a new witch entered. The Witch of Three Horns, carrying a loom shuttle. The Witch of Four Horns, with a skein of black thread. On and on they came, until twelve women sat around the fire, the first with a single horn and the last with a full dozen curling from her brow. They did not speak to the mistress. Instead, they began to work in unison, carding, spinning, and weaving, all the while singing an ancient rhyme in a language the woman did not recognize. Their voices were high and thin, like wind through dead reeds. The mistress tried to rise, but her limbs would not obey. The spell of the witches held her frozen in her chair. She tried to cry out, but no sound left her throat. The twelfth witch, the one with twelve horns, looked up from her weaving and said, "Rise, woman, and make us a cake." The mistress's body moved against her will, walking to the kitchen. She searched for a vessel to fetch water from the well, but every bucket and cup had been hidden. "Go to the well," the twelve-horned witch commanded, "and when you reach the north corner of the house, cry out three times: 'The mountain of the Fenian women and the sky over it is all on fire!'" The mistress stumbled outside. The night was black as pitch, and the cold bit through her thin dress. She reached the north corner of the house and, in desperation, shouted the words as she had been commanded. A voice spoke from the well. It was the Spirit of the Well, old as the hills, wise as running water. "Do as they say," the spirit whispered. "Take the water, bake the cake. But when they are not looking, do this: take the water that washed your children's feet and sprinkle it on the threshold. Take the cake they have made from meal mixed with blood, and place a piece in each sleeper's mouth to break the enchantment. Take the cloth they have woven and hang it half in and half out of the chest. And bar the door behind them when they leave." The mistress returned to the house and baked the cake. When the twelve witches demanded more water, she pretended to comply but instead performed every task the spirit had commanded. Then, with a最后的 burst of will, she cried out, "The mountain of the Fenian women and the sky over it is all on fire!" The twelve witches howled in fury. They rushed from the house, their horns catching the firelight, their grey cloaks streaming behind them, and fled across the dark fields toward Slievenamon. They never returned. The mistress barred the door, fed her family the blessed bread, and locked the enchanted cloth half in and half out of the chest. The mantle dropped by the fleeing witches was hung on the wall as a sign of that terrible night, and it remained in the family for five hundred years.
moral: Cunning and courage defeat even the darkest magic.

source_references.title: Ancient Legends, Mystic Charms, and Superstitions of Ireland
source_references.author: Lady Wilde
source_references.note: Lady Wilde's account of the Horned Women (bean sídhe variants) in the Irish folk tradition.

title: The Holy Grail
summary: At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other.
full_text:
At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other. As they waited for a marvel to occur, a sudden clap of thunder shook the castle. A ray of sunlight brighter than any that had ever pierced the windows flooded the hall, and there, floating above the Round Table, appeared the Holy Grail, covered in white samite so radiant that no one could look directly at it. It filled the hall with the most exquisite aromas and placed before each knight the food and drink he most desired. Then it vanished, leaving the knights stunned and yearning.

Sir Gawain, the first to speak, rose and swore an oath: "I will go on the quest of the Holy Grail for a year and a day, and I will not return until I have seen it more clearly." One by one, every knight of the Round Table swore the same. King Arthur, seeing his fellowship about to scatter, wept. "You have undone me," he said. "The greatest fellowship the world has ever known will be broken by this quest."

The knights rode out across Britain, each following his own path. Many failed. Sir Gawain, brave but worldly, could not overcome his pride. Sir Lancelot, the greatest knight of all, was barred from the Grail's full revelation because of his adulterous love for Queen Guinevere. He entered the chamber where the Grail was kept, but a voice told him he could not enter further. He was struck down for three weeks and nearly died.

Only three knights succeeded. The first was Sir Galahad, the son of Lancelot, who had been raised in purity by nuns. From his first day at Camelot, he had sat in the Siege Perilous, the seat at the Round Table that would kill any unworthy knight who dared claim it. Galahad was the perfect knight, untouched by sin, his heart fixed entirely on heaven. He drew a sword from a stone that no other could move, and it was prophesied that he would achieve the Grail.

The second was Sir Perceval, the innocent one, who had been raised in the forest by his mother and knew nothing of knightly ways. His simplicity and faith carried him further than armor ever could. The third was Sir Bors, the only one of the three who would return to Camelot alive. He was flawed but penitent, and his humility earned him a place at the Grail's table.

Their journey led them through enchanted forests and perilous castles, to the court of the Maimed King, whose wound could only be healed by the Grail's presence. At last, in the mystical castle of Corbenic, Galahad was brought before the Holy Vessel. He knelt and received his Savior. Then he prayed that he might leave the world. His wish was granted, and angels bore his soul to heaven. Perceval became a hermit and died in contemplation. Only Bors returned to Camelot to tell the tale.

The Holy Grail was never seen again in Britain. It was taken up to heaven, leaving behind a broken fellowship but also a story of faith, purity, and the search for something holier than earthly glory. The quest for the Grail became the highest ideal of knighthood: to seek, to strive, and to find what lies beyond the reach of swords and crowns.
moral: Purity of heart is required for the sacred.

title: Hanako-San: The Bathroom Ghost
summary: A ghost girl haunts school bathrooms in Japan. Knock three times and ask if she is there—if she answers, you are marked.
full_text:
In the sterile, echoing hallways of Japanese schools, where the scent of floor wax and old chalk lingers in the air, there is a legend spoken only in hurried whispers. It is a story that turns the mundane act of visiting the restroom into a ritual of terror. This is the legend of Hanako-san, the ghost of the girl in the third stall.

The ritual is precise, for the spirits of the school do not tolerate mistakes. To summon her, one must travel to the third floor at the stroke of midnight, when the school is a skeletal remains of its daytime self. You must enter the girls' bathroom, walk past the first and second stalls, and stop before the third. There, you must knock three times on the wooden door—three slow, deliberate thuds that reverberate through the tiled silence. Then, in a voice barely above a whisper, you must ask: "Hanako-san, are you there?"

For most, the answer is silence, and the silence is a mercy. But for some, a voice answers. It is a voice that sounds like a child's, yet carries the weight of a thousand years of sorrow. "Yes," she whispers. "I am here."

At that moment, the air in the bathroom turns freezing, and the lights flicker with an unstable, rhythmic pulse. Some claim to see a pale, small hand emerge from the gap in the stall door, its skin the color of wet ash. Others report seeing a young girl with a sharp, bobbed haircut and a vibrant red skirt, her eyes wide and vacant, staring from a place beyond the veil of the living.

The origins of Hanako-san are as varied as the schools she haunts. In some versions, she was a victim of the Great Air Raids of World War II, a terrified child who hid in the third stall of her school bathroom, only for the building to collapse around her in a rain of fire. In others, she was a student who suffered the cruelties of bullying, her spirit broken by the laughter of her peers until she sought the finality of suicide in the very place she felt most isolated. Some even whisper that she was murdered by a parent or a stranger, her life stolen in a moment of senseless violence.

Regardless of how she arrived, Hanako-san is now a fixture of the urban landscape. She is the manifestation of childhood fear, the embodiment of the secrets we hide in the locked rooms of our lives. In Tokyo, a shrine has even been erected to honor her, where children leave candy and notes, hoping to appease the spirit of the girl who never got to grow up.

But the legend persists. Every year, a new generation of students dares their friends to knock three times and call her name. They do it for the thrill, for the social currency of bravery, unaware that once you call to Hanako-san, you have opened a door that cannot always be closed. For in the silence of the third stall, Hanako-san is always listening, waiting for the next curious soul to ask if she is still there.
moral: Fear lives in the places we avoid—the locked room, the dark.

source_references.title: The Book of Yōkai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
source_references.author: Michael Dylan Foster

source_references.title: Japanese Ghost Stories: Origins of Yūrei and Yōkai
source_references.author: Michael Dylan Foster

title: The Hyena Who Wanted to Be King
summary: In the golden, shimmering plains of the Sahel, where the acacia trees cast long, skeletal shadows across the scorched earth, the animals of the wild once gathered for a great council.
full_text:
In the golden, shimmering plains of the Sahel, where the acacia trees cast long, skeletal shadows across the scorched earth, the animals of the wild once gathered for a great council. Their leader, the old lion, had grown frail, his roar now a mere whisper, and the time had come to choose a new king who could guide the creatures of the savanna with wisdom and justice.

Among those who vied for the throne was Gbaru, a hyena of immense ambition and unsettling charisma. Gbaru did not possess the strength of the elephant or the majesty of the leopard, but he had a tongue that could weave webs of deceit and a laugh that could unsettle the bravest of hearts. He strutted before the assembly, his coat a mottled grey, his eyes glinting with a hunger that had nothing to do with food.

"Why not me?" Gbaru proclaimed, his voice echoing across the plains. "I am the cleverest of all! I can see the wind before it blows and hear the grass grow. I will lead you to the hidden watering holes and protect you from the storms of the desert!"

The animals, intrigued by his confidence, decided to test the candidates. Each was asked a single, pivotal question: "If you are crowned king, what will you do for your people?"

When it was Gbaru's turn, his mask of benevolence slipped. He looked at the smaller animals—the meerkats, the gazelles, the timid hares—and a cruel smile touched his lips. "What will I do for you?" he sneered. "I will kill the weak so that the strong may thrive. I will divide the prey among myself, keeping the choicest cuts of meat while you scavenge for the scraps. I will rule with a fist of iron, and you will learn the beauty of obedience."

A cold silence fell over the council. The animals looked at each other, the realization dawning upon them that Gbaru did not seek to lead them, but to devour them. They turned their gaze toward a small, unassuming figure sitting at the edge of the circle: the tortoise.

The tortoise spoke slowly, his voice like the rustle of dry leaves. "I do not seek the throne for glory," he said. "But if you choose me, I promise this: each of us shall carry the group. The strong shall protect the weak, the wise shall guide the lost, and the abundance of the land shall be shared equally. None shall stand alone, for we are one body, one spirit."

The animals chose the tortoise, and for many seasons, the Sahel knew a peace it had never seen. Gbaru, consumed by rage and humiliation, was banished from the great gatherings. He was forced to hunt alone, his ambitions reduced to a bitter memory.

It is said that this is why, to this very day, the hyena's laugh is a sound of loneliness and spite. They laugh at nothing, for they have no one to share their joy with. They remain on the fringes of the animal kingdom, a perpetual reminder that leadership is not a prize to be seized for self-gain, but a sacred duty of service.
moral: True leaders serve; tyrants rule for themselves.

source_references.title: A Treasury of African Folklore
source_references.author: Harold Courlander
source_references.note: West African animal fables featuring the hyena as a trickster figure, drawn from oral traditions across the Sahel.

title: The Huntress and the Great Serpent
summary: A brave huntress must defeat a great serpent that has been terrorizing her village—and discovers a unexpected truth.
full_text:
In a small village nestled among the rolling hills of Kenya, there lived a tradition born of fear and ancient pacts. Every year, the Great Serpent emerged from the depths of a limestone cave to demand a sacrifice. The serpent was a titan of scales and shadow, its eyes like molten gold and its breath a poisonous mist that could wither a forest in seconds. To appease the beast and ensure the survival of the rest of the village, the elders chose a single maiden to be delivered to the cave.

This year, the lot fell to Amina. But Amina was not the trembling girl the elders expected. She was the village's most skilled huntress, a woman who could track a leopard through a thunderstorm and shoot an arrow through a falling leaf from a hundred paces. While the village wept for her, Amina spent her final days in preparation. She did not pray for mercy; she prayed for strength.

Instead of the traditional flowers and perfumes, Amina carried a wooden dagger, carved from the heart of a lightning-struck acacia tree and blessed by the village's oldest priest. She wore her hunting leathers, her bow slung across her back and her heart beating with a steady, rhythmic courage.

When she entered the cave, the darkness seemed to swallow her whole. The air grew thick with the smell of sulfur and old blood. Then, from the depths of the cavern, the Great Serpent appeared. It was a mountain of iridescent scales, its body coiling around the stalactites like a living river of emerald and gold. The serpent spoke, its voice a vibration that shook the very bones of Amina's body. "Another morsel," it hissed. "Another life to feed the hunger of the deep."

But as the serpent lunged, Amina did not scream. She dove beneath the creature's massive head, her movements a blur of predatory grace. With a cry of defiance, she plunged the wooden dagger into the serpent's heart.

A blinding light erupted from the wound, filling the cave with a warmth that felt like a thousand suns. The serpent did not die in agony; instead, its form began to dissolve, the scales falling away like autumn leaves. From the shimmering remains emerged a man—old, frail, and dressed in the tattered robes of a forgotten era.

"Thank you," the man whispered, his voice trembling with a relief that had spanned centuries. "I was the guardian of these hills, cursed by a jealous rival to live as a monster until a heart of pure bravery could find the strength to kill me. You have not only saved yourself, Amina; you have liberated a soul."

The man vanished into a pillar of golden light, leaving behind a single, glowing gemstone that ensured the village's prosperity for generations. Amina returned to her people not as a victim, but as a conqueror. She taught them that fear is a cage we build for ourselves, and that sometimes, the monsters we dread are merely the cursed, waiting for someone brave enough to offer them the gift of freedom.
moral: Not all monsters are evil—some seek rescue.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Bantu
source_references.author: Alice Werner

title: Hanuman
summary: Hanuman was born to the wind god Vayu and the celestial nymph Anjana.. According to the Ramayana, his mother was a vanara — a divine monkey being — who had been blessed with a son possessing the strength of the gods.
full_text:
Hanuman was born to the wind god Vayu and the celestial nymph Anjana. According to the Ramayana, his mother was a vanara — a divine monkey being — who had been blessed with a son possessing the strength of the gods. When Hanuman was still a child, he mistook the rising sun for a fruit and leaped into the sky to catch it. Indra, king of the gods, struck him with his thunderbolt, angering Vayu so greatly that the wind god withdrew all air from the universe. The gods, suffocating, quickly blessed Hanuman with multiple boons: invulnerability to weapons, the power to change his size at will, and the ability to fly.

Years later, when the exiled prince Rama and his brother Lakshmana wandered the forest in search of Rama's kidnapped wife Sita, they encountered the monkey king Sugriva, who sent Hanuman to investigate them. Hanuman approached in the guise of a humble Brahmin and soon pledged his service to Rama.

The quest to find Sita led Hanuman to leap across the ocean to Lanka — a jump of a hundred leagues. He shrank to the size of a cat and crept through the demon city until he found Sita in Ashoka Vatika, a garden of golden trees. He revealed himself, offered Rama's ring as proof, and promised rescue.

But before he could leave, Hanuman was captured by Ravana's soldiers. The demon king ordered his tail wrapped in oil-soaked cloth and set aflame. Hanuman miraculously escaped, and as he leaped from rooftop to rooftop, his burning tail set much of Lanka ablaze. He then extinguished his tail in the sea and returned to Rama with the news.

Hanuman became the commander of Rama's army of monkeys and bears. During the great battle of Lanka, he flew to the Himalayas to fetch the Sanjivani herb to revive the wounded Lakshmana. Unable to identify the correct herb, he lifted the entire mountain and carried it back to the battlefield. After Rama's victory, Hanuman continued to serve as a devoted disciple. To this day, he is worshipped across India as the symbol of strength, devotion, and selfless service.
moral: Service is its own reward.

source_references.title: The Ramayana
source_references.author: R.K. Narayan (retold from Valmiki)
source_references.note: Accessible retelling of the Valmiki Ramayana preserving the classical Hanuman narratives including the burning of Lanka and the Himalayan herb search.

title: Hou Yi and the Arrows
summary: Before there were emperors or dynasties, before history was written in bronze and bone, the world was ruled by gods, and among those gods were the ten suns.
full_text: Before there were emperors or dynasties, before history was written in bronze and bone, the world was ruled by gods, and among those gods were the ten suns. They were the children of the Jade Emperor and the goddess Xihe, beings of pure flame who lived in a giant mulberry tree at the edge of the eastern sea. Each morning, one sun would climb into the sky, travel west across the heavens, and descend into the branches of another mulberry tree in the west, while the other nine rested in the eastern tree. This was the natural order, and for millennia, it worked perfectly. But the ten suns grew bored with their solitary journeys. They wanted to experience the world together. So one day, all ten climbed into the sky at once. The effect was immediate and catastrophic. The earth began to char. Rivers boiled away to nothing. The forests of the east burst into flames, and the deserts of the west turned to glass. In the north, the ice caps melted and flooded the plains. In the south, the jungles dried up and turned to tinder. Crops withered in the fields, and the people of China, the children of Yan and Huang, began to die. The great archer Hou Yi looked up at the ten blazing suns and felt a cold fury rise in his chest. He was a hunter of unmatched skill, born of divine lineage, whose arrows could split a falling leaf at a thousand paces. He went to the Jade Emperor and volunteered to stop the suns. The Emperor, who had lost control of his children, gave his reluctant permission. Hou Yi climbed to the top of the Kunlun Mountains, the highest peak in the world, where the air is thin and the sky seems close enough to touch. He carried a bow made from the horn of a celestial ram and arrows fletched with the feathers of the roc. He planted his feet on a granite outcrop, drew a deep breath, and nocked his first arrow. "Suns of heaven," he called, his voice carrying on the wind, "return to your tree, and I will spare you!" The suns laughed. Their combined heat blistered Hou Yi's skin, but he did not flinch. He drew the bowstring to his cheek, aimed at the nearest sun, and released. The arrow flew straight and true. It pierced the sun's flaming heart, and the sun fell from the sky, trailing fire and smoke, crashing into the eastern sea with a hiss that sent steam billowing across the land. It became a three-legged crow, the sacred sun-bird, and its fire was extinguished forever. Hou Yi nocked a second arrow. A second sun fell. Then a third, a fourth, a fifth. The sky darkened as the suns plummeted one by one. The people on earth looked up through tearing eyes and saw the great archer standing on the mountain peak, a black silhouette against the blinding light, shooting down the gods themselves. On the ninth arrow, the Emperor Yao, who ruled the mortal realm, came running up the mountain. "Stop!" he cried, grabbing Hou Yi's arm. "Leave one sun, or the world will be plunged into eternal darkness, and we will all freeze!" Hou Yi lowered his bow. He had shot down nine suns. The tenth, terrified beyond measure, fled to the far edge of the sky and hid behind a cave. The world was plunged into cold and darkness. The surviving sun ignored all pleas to return. Only when the rooster climbed to the highest rooftop and crowed "Brother! Brother!" did the sun peer out from its hiding place. It has been rising every morning since, at the rooster's call, too frightened ever to stay away. For his deeds, the Queen Mother of the West rewarded Hou Yi with an elixir of immortality. But Hou Yi did not drink it. He gave it to his wife, Chang Er, for safekeeping, unwilling to gain immortality without her. This decision would lead to the most famous tragedy in Chinese mythology, but that is another story. For his heroism, Hou Yi is remembered as the greatest archer who ever lived, the savior of a world that was burning, the man who dared to shoot down the sun.
moral: Great power must be matched by great wisdom.

title: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be
summary: An etiological tale explaining why Småland is rocky and barren while Skåne is flat and fertile — because the Devil tried to create land but was foiled at every turn by God. This humorous origin legend was also used by Selma Lagerlöf in her writings.
full_text:
HOW SMALAND AND SCHONEN CAME TO BE
     XXIII
moral: God's creation is perfect; the Devil's imitations always fall short.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXII: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. An etiological legend — the Danes and Swedes would say the Devil created these regions, a motif also used by Selma Lagerlöf.

source_references.title: Svenska folksagor
source_references.author: Hyltén-Cavallius, Gunnar Olof and Stephens, George

title: He Who Could Ride in All Weathers
summary: A castle-dwelling daughter is brought home by her father to the castle in Tyrol. She does not give her hand to the richest or most handsome suitor, but instead chooses the one who defies all dangers and makes his way through all weathers. The tale carries a feminist message about a woman's right to choose her partner based on character rather than wealth or status.
full_text: A young woman of noble birth living in a castle in Tyrol is brought home by her father, who wishes to see her properly married. Suitors arrive from far and wide — the richest lords, the handsomest knights, the most powerful noblemen — each presenting their wealth, titles, and grand estates. But the daughter looks past all their finery. She watches how each man conducts himself, noticing who takes shelter from the storm and who braves it. She does not give her hand to the richest, most handsome, or most powerful suitor. Instead, she chooses the one who defies all dangers and makes his way through all weathers — a man of true character who proves his worth through action rather than boasting. The tale carries a subtle but clear feminist message about a woman's right to choose her own partner based on the content of his character rather than his wealth, title, or social status.
moral: Character is revealed not by fair weather but by the storms one is willing to face.

source_references.title: The Eighteenth Century Scandinavian Folktale Cycle
source_references.author: Bergman, G.
source_references.publisher: Samfundet för Svensk Folkt Forskning, Stockholm

title: How One Went Out to Woo
summary: A clumsy but well-meaning man attempts to find a wife, encountering a series of absurd obstacles and eccentric characters. Through sheer luck and a refusal to be discouraged, he eventually finds a partner who appreciates his oddities.
full_text: Once there was a man who decided he was tired of loneliness and went out to woo a bride. He was not a handsome man, nor was he wealthy, but he had a heart full of hope and a tendency to trip over his own feet. He visited the first village and proposed to the daughter of the mayor, but he accidentally knocked over the wedding cake and was chased out with a broom. He visited the second village and attempted to impress a lady with a poem, but he forgot the words and started singing a lullaby to a goat instead. Just as he was about to give up, he met a woman who was just as clumsy as he was; she had accidentally set fire to her own kitchen while trying to bake bread. They looked at each other, laughed at their shared misfortunes, and realized they were perfect for one another. They married in a small ceremony where the cake was slightly lopsided, but the love was perfectly straight.
moral: You don't need to be perfect to find someone who loves you; you just need to find someone whose imperfections match your own.

source_references.title: The Types of the Norwegian Folktale
source_references.author: Krohn, J.

title: The Honest Penny
summary: A poor boy finds a money box but throws it away, believing it contains stolen wealth, keeping only one silver penny that floats on water, which he deems 'honest.' This act of integrity leads him to a series of adventures and eventual immense wealth.
full_text: Once on a time there was a poor woman who lived in a tumble-down hut. She sent her little boy into the wood to gather fuel. He found a white crooked stone and, thinking it was frozen, covered it with his jacket. Beneath the stone, he found a money-box full of silver. Believing it was stolen, he threw it into a tarn, but one silver penny floated on the top. 'This is honest,' the lad said, and kept it. Later, he worked as a scullion for a merchant. The merchant bought a cat for the lad's penny. This cat proved invaluable, helping the merchant clear mice from foreign lands and earning him huge sums of money. Ultimately, the merchant gave all the wealth back to the lad, who married the merchant's daughter and lived in luxury.
moral: True honesty is rewarded in the end, even when it seems foolish or impractical to others.

title: Helhest
summary: A three-legged horse associated with the goddess Hel. It is seen in churchyards as a harbinger of death and illness, often appearing before a person dies. Legend says a living horse was interred in every churchyard before human burial, returning as the Hel-horse.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of Helhest is told. A three-legged horse associated with the goddess Hel. It is seen in churchyards as a harbinger of death and illness, often appearing before a person dies. Legend says a living horse was interred in every churchyard before human burial, returning as the Hel-horse. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Life is full of mysteries that demand our respect.

title: The Huldra's Seduction
summary: The Huldra is a beautiful forest woman who lures lonely men into the woods with her singing and charm. While she appears human from the front, she possesses a long cow's tail which she carefully hides. If a man discovers the tail and treats her with kindness, she may become a loyal wife, but otherwise, she leads men to their doom.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Huldra's Seduction has been told for generations. The Huldra is a beautiful forest woman who lures lonely men into the woods with her singing and charm. While she appears human from the front, she possesses a long cow's tail which she carefully hides. If a man discovers the tail and treats her with kindness, she may become a loyal wife, but otherwise, she leads men to their doom. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Hidden Paths of the Vittra
summary: The Vittra are invisible underground beings who live in parallel cities. They have their own cattle and roads that cross human lands. If a farmer builds a house over a Vittra-path, the invisible residents will cause accidents, make the cows sick, or drive the family out of the home through relentless noise.

The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.

title: The Hunger of the Amarok
summary: In this darker version of the wolf legend, the Amarok is a sinister force that hunts those who disturb the balance of nature. A man, consumed by grief, seeks out the Amarok to distract himself. After killing the wolf's young to lure the adult, the man is swiftly judged. The Amarok returns and takes the man's soul as punishment for his reckless cruelty toward the natural world.
full_text: After losing his family to a winter storm, a hunter lost his grip on the world. His grief curdled into rage, and rage into a hunger for destruction. He wandered the tundra with no purpose but to cause pain. 'If I can kill the Amarok,' he said, 'I will have conquered death itself.' He tracked the wolf to a remote valley where the snow never melted. There he found its den and two cubs, blind and helpless. The hunter killed them and stacked their bodies at the entrance, knowing the mother would come. The Amarok returned at dusk, a mountain of gray fur and silent fury. She did not attack. She simply looked at the hunter with eyes that held no anger, only judgment. Then she opened her mouth and drew in a breath that pulled at the hunter's soul. He crumpled to the ground. He did not die physically. He walked back to his village, ate, slept, and spoke. But his eyes were empty, and his hands never again held a weapon. He lived out his years as a hollow shell.
moral: Those who seek to destroy nature for revenge ultimately destroy themselves.

title: How Fox Saved the People
summary: During a catastrophic famine, the cunning Fox discovers a hidden source of food. While Raven tries to take credit through tricks, Fox's genuine assistance saves the community. The tale serves as a lesson in humility and the value of practical wisdom over empty boasting.
full_text: A famine struck with ferocity. Raven saw an opportunity. 'I have found food! A valley rich with berries beyond the mountains!' The people followed, struggling through storms. The valley was bare. Raven laughed and flew away. Fox, watching from a ridge, padded down. 'I know where the food is,' she said quietly. She led them to a hidden cove where currents had trapped a school of fish. The people gorged themselves. Fox stayed until the famine passed. When Raven returned and tried to claim credit, the people laughed. 'We know who truly saved us,' they said. 'It was the quiet one, not the loud one.'
moral: Quiet competence and genuine help are worth more than loud boasting and empty promises.

title: How the Stálos Were Tricked
summary: A classic Stállu trickster tale where a clever Sámi outwits one or more Stállu giants through cunning rather than strength. The story exemplifies the core pattern of Sámi Stállu narratives: a small, clever Sámi hero uses intelligence and knowledge of the landscape to defeat the much larger, stronger, but stupider Stállu. The Stállu's own knife must never be used against him as it gives him strength. After defeating Stállu, the hero must also kill Stállu's dog Rakke, which can lick wounds and resurrect its master.
full_text: A Sámi hunter was returning to his camp when he found a Stállu blocking the path. 'I will eat you,' said the Stállu. The hunter, whose name is forgotten but whose cleverness is remembered, smiled. 'Before you eat me,' he said, 'let me show you something.' He led the Stállu to a frozen lake and pointed at the reflection of the moon. 'Do you see that cheese? It is the most delicious thing in the world. If you eat it, you will never be hungry again.' The Stállu lay down on the ice and reached for the moon's reflection. The hunter pushed him into the water. But the Stállu's dog Rakke licked his master's wounds and brought him back to life. The hunter remembered the old wisdom: never use the Stállu's own knife against him, for it gives him strength. He used his own knife instead and killed Rakke first, then the Stállu. The story teaches that a Stállu must never be fought with his own weapons, and his dog must always be dealt with first.
moral: Never fight an enemy with their own weapons, and always deal with their helpers first.
source: Qvigstad & Saba, Lappiske eventyr og sagn; Stallo Wikipedia; levandekulturarv.se

title: Čáhcerávga: The Water Wraith
summary: Unlike the Rávga (believed real), Čáhcerávga (Water Wraith) was known to be a fictional creature used to scare children away from dangerous waters. It was described as a long-necked, scary creature that might carry a child away. It was said to appear in all bodies of water where children were not supposed to go, including wells.
full_text: Čáhcerávga lives in every dangerous body of water in Sápmi. It has a long, thin neck like a snake and eyes that glow in the dark. Its fingers are cold as the deepest fjord, and its grip is unbreakable. The Sámi parents knew the truth about Čáhcerávga: they made it up. The water wraith was not a real spirit like the rávga—it was a story invented to keep children safe from drowning. 'Do not go near the river,' parents warned. 'Čáhcerávga will carry you away.' 'Do not play near the well. Čáhcerávga lives there.' The children believed, and the children stayed safe. As they grew older, they understood the truth and told the same story to their own children. In a land of freezing rivers, hidden wells, and iceberg-laden fjords, a fictional monster saved more lives than any real one ever could.
moral: Sometimes a lie told for the right reason protects more than the truth ever could.

title: How Pieraš Ánde Learned Noaidi Skills
summary: The initiation story of a Sámi noaidi named Pieraš Ánde who learned shamanic arts. The noaidi's calling often came through dreams, where an ancestor or ancient drum would call the initiate. The initiation involved withdrawal into solitude, metaphorical death and rebirth, and learning to navigate the three levels of the universe to retrieve souls, heal, and divine the future.
full_text: Pieraš Ánde was an ordinary herder until the dreams began. In his sleep, an old man appeared—a noaidi from long ago, his face lined with wisdom and his eyes bright with the light of other worlds. The old man showed Pieraš Ánde the sacred drum, painted with the symbols of the upper, middle, and lower worlds. 'You are called,' the old man said. 'You must answer.' Pieraš Ánde withdrew from his village and went into the wilderness. He fasted for nine days under the open sky, his body growing thin, his mind growing clear. On the ninth day, he died. Not his body, but his old self. A noaidi must die as who they were to be reborn as who they must become. In his vision, he traveled to the upper world, where Beaivi lives. He traveled to the underworld, where Jábmiidáhkká rules. He met helping spirits—a bear, a salmon, a reindeer—who agreed to serve him. When he returned to his body, he was changed. He could see the spirits that moved invisibly among the living. He could beat the drum and read its patterns. He could retrieve lost souls and heal the sick. Pieraš Ánde became a great noaidi, but he never forgot the nine days he spent dying and being reborn under the Arctic sky.
moral: To heal others, one must first die to their old self and be reborn through vision.
source: Qvigstad & Saba; laits.utexas.edu (Noaidi and Inuit Angakoq)

title: Huldufólk (The Hidden People)
summary: Huldufólk are supernatural beings in Faroese folklore described as large-built, grey-clad, dark-haired people who live in mounds, rocks, and cliffs—invisible to humans unless they choose to be seen. They are tricksters who dislike electricity, technology, crosses, and churches. According to legend, they left Suðuroy when electricity arrived in the 1950s. The Hidden People feature in Faroese nursery rhymes (skjaldur) such as 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' and traditional kvæði (ballads). They are said to fear knives and can be warded off with the sign of the cross. They coexist in a parallel society with farms, churches, and social structures mirroring human communities.

title: Huldufólk Captured by a Priest at Sørvágsvatn
summary: At Sørvágsvatn Lake on Vágar (the lake that famously appears to hang over the ocean), there is a stone mound where, according to legend, a priest trapped several Huldufólk (Hidden People). The priest used his spiritual authority to imprison the supernatural beings within the mound. Visitors to the area claim they can still hear the wailing and cries of the trapped hidden people emanating from the stone. The legend reflects Faroese beliefs about the power of Christian clergy over pagan supernatural beings, and the common motif of Huldufólk being afraid of crosses, churches, and Christian symbols.

title: The Huldufólk Nursery Rhyme: Hulda, Mulda, Tulda
summary: A traditional Faroese skjaldur (short nursery rhyme or tale) that invokes the Huldufólk. The rhyme 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' is a charm asking the Hidden People not to hide lost objects. It reflects the common belief that Huldufólk, being mischievous tricksters, enjoy hiding household items from humans. The rhyme tradition is one of the few surviving forms of Huldufólk interaction in the modern era—residents of Suðuroy claim the hidden people left with the introduction of electricity in the 1950s. These skjaldur, studied by linguist Stephen Pax Leonard, represent an endangered oral tradition unique to the Faroe Islands, blending pre-Christian and Christian elements.

title: The Hero Twins: Monster Slayer and Child of the Water
summary: In Navajo mythology, Changing Woman gave birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer (Naayééʼ Neizghání) and Child of the Water (Tóbájíshchíní), fathered by the Sun. The twins were born to rid the world of monsters (anaye) that terrorized the people. They journeyed to their father the Sun, passing tests of strength and courage. The Sun tried to kill them with a sweat lodge of stone knives, a heated stone bed, and poisoned smoke, but Spider Woman protected them. They received lightning arrows and armor before returning to earth. Monster Slayer killed the giant Yéʼiitsoh by striking his leg armor and then his head with arrows. He and his brother slew Déélgééd the Horned Monster by shooting arrows into his body and causing him to fall into the ocean. They disposed of other monsters including the giant birds, the monster who kicked people off cliffs, and those who caused hunger, poverty, cold, and old age. Some monsters were allowed to remain in diminished form as a balance for humanity.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Hopi Clan Migrations
summary: Upon arriving in the Fourth World, the Hopi divided and began great migrations across the land. Led by various signs and helped by Spider Woman, clans traveled to every direction, sometimes stopping to build towns, then abandoning them to continue their migrations. They left symbols on rocks to show that Hopi had been there. Clans were named after events or signs received during their journeys. Periods of harmonious living alternated with wickedness, contention, and separation — a pattern that began in the First World. The clans traveled to the farthest extremity of the land in every direction; far to the north was the 'Back Door' (possibly the Bering land bridge), but this was closed to the Hopi, though other peoples came through it. Eventually, guided by Masauwu, the Hopi were led to their permanent home in northeastern Arizona, where Masauwu gave them the land in exchange for their stewardship and service.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis; Ekkehart Malotki, Hopi Ruin Legends

title: Holy Boy Makes the Sun and Moon
summary: In Apache tradition, Holy Boy was unhappy with the darkness in the underworld and decided to create a sun. He tried many materials without success. Cyclone, spying on the Hactcin, told Holy Boy that White Hactcin had the sun. Holy Boy went to White Hactcin who gave him the sun, and to Black Hactcin who gave him the moon. Black Hactcin taught Holy Boy how to make a sacred sandpainting on a buckskin to hold the sun and moon. Holy Boy, Red Boy, Black Hactcin, and White Hactcin held a ceremony with songs, and White Hactcin released the sun while Black Hactcin released the moon. Light grew stronger as the sun moved from north to south, becoming daylight. The medicine people immediately claimed credit for making the sun, arguing with each other. The Hactcin warned them about eclipses. When an eclipse came, the medicine people could not restore the sun — only the Hactcin could, teaching that humans should not claim divine powers.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); nativeamerican-art.com

title: How the Summer Birds Came
summary: A Zuni myth explaining the origin of small summer birds. In the days of the ancients, there were no little birds — only large birds like eagles, crows, and hawks. A Zuni man discovered a magical bundle of feathers left by the spirits. When he walked into a field of flowers, the bundle began to flutter, and small wings flew out from it, becoming the Sacred Birds of Summerland. The feathers were made living by the lives of the birds that had once borne them, and they came to life again in the environment of flowers and summer that they had loved. This is why, according to Zuni tradition, there are jaybirds, sparrows, finches, willow-birds, and all the beautiful little birds that herald summer and hover over flowers. The story concludes with the storyteller noting that Zunis have books too — not with marks but with words in their hearts from the ancients.

title: How the Coyote Joined the Dance of the Burrowing-Owls
summary: A Zuni trickster tale about Coyote who stumbled upon a sacred dance of the Burrowing-owls. The owls were performing a ceremonial dance in the moonlight, and Coyote, ever the opportunist, wanted to join. At first the owls were suspicious, but Coyote insisted he could dance as well as they. He joined their circle, but his clumsy, undisciplined dancing disrupted the sacred ceremony. The owls, annoyed by his foolish behavior, played a trick on him. When Coyote tired and wanted to leave, he found that the owls' dance had placed him under a spell — he could not stop dancing. He was forced to dance until dawn, exhausted and humiliated. The story explains why coyotes are often seen running in circles or acting strangely at night. It teaches the Zuni lesson that not all dances are for everyone and that sacred ceremonies require proper preparation and respect.

source_references.publisher: Publications of the Academy of Pacific Coast History, Vol. 8

title: How Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma Stole the Thunder-Stone and Lightning-Shaft
summary: In this Zuni myth, the twin war gods Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma acquired the powerful weapons of thunder and lightning. The thunder-stone and lightning-shaft were kept by powerful beings who did not want to share their power. The twins, known for their cunning and bravery, devised a plan to steal these sacred weapons. Using their shapeshifting abilities and the help of their grandmother (Spider Woman often fills this role), they infiltrated the home of the thunder beings. They stole the thunder-stone (which creates thunder when struck) and the lightning-shaft (which shoots bolts of lightning). These weapons made them invincible in battle and allowed them to defeat enemies too powerful for ordinary weapons. The acquisition of thunder and lightning represents the transfer of cosmic power to the heroes who protect the Zuni people and maintain order in the world.

source_references.title: Arikara Traditions
source_references.author: Gladys A. Reichard
source_references.publisher: Columbia University Contributions to Anthropology, Vol. XIV

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Northern Plains
source_references.author: Charles A. Eastman (Ohiyesa)

title: The Hermit Mitsina
summary: A Zuni story about Mitsina, a hermit who lived in seclusion with spiritual power. Mitsina had withdrawn from human society to live in a remote cave, where he communed with the spirits and gained deep knowledge of the sacred world. When the people faced a crisis, they sought him out for his wisdom and power. Mitsina, despite his isolation, felt compassion for his people and agreed to help. He used his spiritual knowledge to perform ceremonies and prayers that resolved the village's problems. Afterward, he withdrew again to his cave, teaching that spiritual power requires solitude and discipline to maintain. The story presents the Zuni concept of the shaman or medicine person who lives between worlds — part of the human community yet separate, possessing power that comes from sacrifice and isolation.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Crow Indians
source_references.author: Robert Lowie
source_references.publisher: Anthropological Papers of the American Museum of Natural History, Vol. 2, Part 1

title: The How the Twins of War and Chance Fared with the Unborn-Made Men
summary: In this complex Zuni myth, Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma, the Twins of War and Chance (or Morning Star and Evening Star), journeyed to the underworld to confront the Unborn-made Men. These were unfinished beings who had not yet been given their final form — they existed in a state of potential. The twins had to navigate the dark underworld, using their wits and divine weapons. They encountered various spirits and obstacles, including the guardians of the underworld. The story explores themes of destiny, free will, and the nature of creation — why some beings are completed and others are not. The twins' journey through the underworld mirrors the soul's journey after death and the process by which spirits become ancestors. The Unborn-made Men represent the untold potential that exists in every being before it takes its final form in the daylight world.

source_references.title: Calendar History of the Kiowa Indians
source_references.author: James Mooney
source_references.publisher: Seventeenth Annual Report of the Bureau of Ethnology, Part 1, Washington, D.C.

title: How the Rattlesnakes Came to Be What They Are
summary: A Zuni story explaining the origin of rattlesnakes and their relationship with humans. In the days of the ancients, rattlesnakes did not have their distinctive rattle or their dangerous venom. A man who had been saved by a rattlesnake or had made a pact with one became the founder of the Rattlesnake Society. The snake was given its rattle as a warning device so that humans could hear it and avoid being bitten. The venom was given as a defense but also carries healing power in the hands of those who know the proper ceremonies. The story teaches that rattlesnakes are not merely dangerous creatures but sacred beings with a role in the spiritual ecology of the world. Members of the Rattlesnake Society can handle rattlesnakes without harm and use them in healing ceremonies. The rattle is understood as a gift — a way for the snake to say 'I am here, please step carefully.'

source_references.title: Handbook of the Indians North of Mexico
source_references.author: John Wesley Powell (ed.)
source_references.publisher: Bureau of American Ethnology, Bulletin 30, Part 2

title: The Happy Hunting Ground
summary: The Lakota believe the soul journeys after death to the happy hunting ground, a realm resembling the living world with better weather and more plentiful animals that are easier to hunt. Some traditions say the soul travels along the Sky-Road (Milky Way) to reach this destination. At the entrance, the deceased must present proper tattoos to Hihankara (Owl-Maker). Those with correct tattoos are admitted; those without are pushed back to Earth to wander as restless ghosts. The dead may also travel along the Spirit Path, another name for the Milky Way, to reach their final resting place among the stars.

source_references.title: Indian Story and Song from the North American Plains
source_references.author: Alice C. Fletcher

title: How the Buffalo Were Released on Earth
summary: Long ago, all the buffalo were kept hidden in a cave deep beneath the earth by a powerful spirit who did not want to share them with humans. A young Comanche warrior, guided by a wolf spirit, discovered the cave entrance. Following the wolf's advice, he transformed into a buffalo calf and entered the herd. When the keeper opened the cave door to let the buffalo graze, the warrior-turned-calf stampeded the herd out of the cave. The buffalo poured across the plains, and the Comanche people had food and clothing. The wolf spirit taught the people how to hunt the buffalo with respect.
source: Native Languages of the Americas / Comanche folklore

source_references.title: Cheyenne Traditions
source_references.author: G. A. Dorsey and Alfred L. Kroeber

title: The Hako (Calumet) Ceremony
summary: The Hako ceremony, also known as the Calumet ceremony, was a complex Pawnee ritual centered on the sacred pipe. It was a peace-making and adoption ceremony that created bonds between individuals, families, and even tribes. The ceremony lasted several days and involved singing, dancing, smoking the sacred pipe, and the exchange of gifts. A decorated pipe (the Hako) served as the central symbol of the ritual. The ceremony was dedicated to Atira (Mother Corn) and involved a corn symbol painted to represent the Rainstorm, Thunder, Lightning, and Wind. The Hako reinforced social ties and ensured the continuity of the community.

source_references.author: G. A. Dorsey

title: How Medicine Man Resurrected Buffalo
summary: During a terrible famine, all the buffalo had disappeared from the plains. The Arapaho people were starving. A powerful medicine man went into the mountains to seek a vision. After four days of fasting, the spirit of the Earth came to him and told him that the buffalo had gone underground because the people had been wasteful. Following the spirit's instructions, the medicine man gathered the tribe for a ceremony. Through songs, prayers, and the use of his medicine bundle, he opened a passage to the underground buffalo world. The buffalo emerged, and the people were saved. He taught them never to waste the buffalo's gift.

source_references.title: Ethnography of the Hidatsa
source_references.author: Alfred G. Bowers
source_references.publisher: North Dakota Historical Society Studies, Vol. 14

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Northern Plains
source_references.author: Charles A. Eastman (Ohiyesa)

title: Hotuiyi (Antelope) and the Warning
summary: A Comanche hunting party was pursuing a herd of antelope across the plains when a strange antelope with glowing eyes appeared. The lead hunter recognized it as a spirit messenger and halted the chase. The antelope led the party to a hidden spring where they found water, then traced a path around a dangerous canyon where enemy warriors lay in ambush. The hunter realized the antelope had saved them from both thirst and attack. The Comanche gave thanks to the antelope spirit and thereafter considered antelope sightings as omens—if the antelope runs east, good fortune follows; if west, danger approaches.

source_references.title: Hopi Tales
source_references.author: Frank Waters and Oswald White Bear Fredericks

title: Hiawatha and the Invention of Wampum
summary: When Hiawatha's three daughters were killed by the evil Tadodaho, he was consumed by grief and wandered the wilderness. The Great Peacemaker found him and taught him the Condolence Ceremony to ease his grief. As part of this ceremony, Hiawatha invented wampum — strings of purple and white shell beads — to record the laws and treaties of the growing Confederacy. He strung the white beads to represent peace and the purple to represent the grave matters of the law. The strings of wampum became the constitutional record of the Haudenosaunee, serving as mnemonic devices to help storytellers recall the exact words of the Great Law. The Hiawatha Belt, with its central symbols, depicts the five nations united under the Great Tree of Peace.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois; Wikipedia - Great Law of Peace; native-languages.org Peacemaker

source_references.title: Tuscarora Tales
source_references.author: Percy Tarbell
source_references.publisher: Museum of the American Indian, Heye Foundation

source_references.author: Bruce Trigger (ed.)

title: Hi-nun and the Niagara Legend
summary: The Seneca tell of Hi-nun, the Thunderer, who fought a great battle with a monstrous serpent living in the Niagara River. The serpent had grown so large that it blocked the flow of the river, causing devastating floods. Hi-nun descended from the sky with his thunderbolt and struck the serpent, whose thrashing body carved out the shape of Niagara Falls as it died. The mist that rises from the falls is said to be the serpent's dying breath. The Spirit of the West Wind was honored thereafter by the Seneca for his role in the battle, and the thunder of the falls is Hi-nun's voice warning away other monsters. This legend explains both the origin of the falls and the reverence paid to the thunder beings.
source: Smith - Myths of the Iroquois (Gutenberg); Seneca oral tradition

source_references.title: Tuscarora Tales
source_references.author: Percy Tarbell
source_references.publisher: Museum of the American Indian, Heye Foundation

title: How Fisher Went to the Skyland (The Big Dipper)
summary: Long ago, the giant lynx Mishipeshu had imprisoned all the summer birds in a cave beneath the earth, plunging the world into endless winter. The animals held a council and decided to free the birds. Fisher, the swiftest of them, led a band of animals to the cave. While the others distracted Mishipeshu, Fisher slipped inside and released the birds. Summer returned, but the enraged Mishipeshu pursued Fisher across the sky. As Fisher ran, the spirits lifted him higher and higher into the heavens, where he was transformed into the constellation we call the Big Dipper (which the Ojibwe call Ojiig, meaning Fisher). Every year, when the constellation appears in the northern sky, it signals the return of spring. The other animals who helped were also placed in the sky as stars.
source: native-languages.org Ojibwe Legends; Johnston - Mishomis Book

source_references.author: N. N. Winchell (attributed to Hands Lake)

source_references.author: Bruce Trigger (ed.)

title: The Huron Creation: Aataentsic and the Earth Diver
summary: In the beginning, the Wyandot people lived in the Sky Land. The shaman told the people to dig around the roots of the wild apple tree by the chief's lodge. As they dug, they broke through the floor of the Sky Land, and the tree and the chief's daughter Aataentsic fell through. Below was only water. Swans swimming on the water heard a thunderclap — the first thunder ever heard — and looked up to see the woman falling. They swam together so she could land on their backs. The animals held a council called by the Great Turtle. One by one the divers went down — Otter, Muskrat, Beaver — seeking earth from the tree's roots. Each drowned. Finally, Old Toad volunteered. The animals laughed at her smallness, but she dove and surfaced near death with grains of earth in her mouth. The Small Turtle spread the earth around the Big Turtle's shell, where it grew until it became the island of the world.
source: Barbeau, Marius - Huron and Wyandot Mythology (1915); sacred-texts.com; Wikisource Wyandot creation

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 15: Northeast
source_references.author: Bruce Trigger (ed.)

title: Hinnon, the Thunder Spirit
summary: The Huron believe in the Thunders, a group of powerful storm spirits who live in the sky and look like a cross between men and giant turkeys. Their leader is Hinnon, whom they call Grandfather. Hinnon and his warriors pursue and destroy the great horned serpents and other monsters that threaten the world. When lightning flashes, it is Hinnon throwing his thunderbolt; when thunder rolls, it is his voice. The Huron make offerings of tobacco to Hinnon before long journeys and during storms. In the Huron creation myth, the first thunderclap occurred when Aataentsic fell through the Sky Land, marking the beginning of the new world below. The Thunderers also gave the people tobacco and taught them the proper ceremonies to honor the Great Spirit.
source: Barbeau - Huron and Wyandot Mythology; native-languages.org Huron/Wyandot

source_references.author: Silas Tertius Rand

source_references.title: The Algonquin Legends of New England
source_references.author: Charles Godfrey Leland

title: How Gluskabe Stole Tobacco
summary: In the early days, tobacco was guarded jealously by Grasshopper (or a giant), who kept it hidden from humanity. The people prayed for tobacco to use in their ceremonies, as it was the sacred plant that carried their prayers to the Creator. Gluskabe heard their pleas and devised a plan. He transformed himself into a small, harmless creature — sometimes a rabbit, sometimes a mouse — and snuck into Grasshopper's lodge while he was away. He found the tobacco bundled in a pouch and escaped with it. Grasshopper pursued but Gluskabe was too swift. He distributed the tobacco among the Wabanaki tribes, teaching them how to plant and harvest it and how to offer it in sacred pipes. The smoke rising from the pipes carries the people's prayers directly to Gichi Niwaskw, the Great Spirit, and Gluskabe is honored for this gift that connects the people with the divine.

source_references.title: Glooskap the Divinity, and Other Tales of the Micmacs
source_references.author: Silas Tertius Rand

title: How Glooscap Found the Summer
summary: In the long-ago time, there was no summer — only endless winter. The earth was covered in snow and ice, the people were freezing, and the animals suffered. Glooscap decided to find the spirit of summer and bring it back. He traveled to the far north where Winter, a powerful giant, held Summer captive in his ice lodge. Glooscap challenged Winter to a contest of power. They told stories for many days, and as Winter talked, frost grew thicker on the walls. But Glooscap's stories were of warmth, green leaves, and flowing water. He so charmed the spirit of Summer that she awakened and began to melt Winter's ice. Winter retreated north, and Summer followed Glooscap back to the people. Ever since, Summer and Winter alternate — Winter rules for half the year, but when his power wanes, Summer returns and the world thaws. The Mi'kmaq celebrate this cycle as the eternal rhythm of life.
source: native-languages.org Mi'kmaq Legends; Rand - Micmac legends

source_references.title: Glooskap the Divinity, and Other Tales of the Micmacs
source_references.author: Silas Tertius Rand

source_references.publisher: Anthropological Series, Field Museum of Natural History

title: Heloha and Melatha: Thunder and Lightning Birds (Choctaw)
summary: In Choctaw mythology, Heloha (Thunder) and Melatha (Lightning) are two enormous birds responsible for the dramatic thunderstorms that sweep across the Mississippi region. Heloha, the female, lays her giant eggs high in the clouds, and the eggs rumble and roll around atop the clouds, creating the sound of thunder. Her mate, Melatha, is incredibly fast despite his huge size. As he streaks across the sky, he leaves a trail of sparks that become lightning. Together they create the powerful storms that bring rain to the land. The Choctaw respected these bird spirits and understood thunder and lightning as natural phenomena caused by living creatures, not abstract forces.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Southeastern Indians
source_references.author: John R. Swanton

title: The Hunter of the Sun (Choctaw)
summary: None of the Choctaw prophets or chief leaders could answer what happened to the Sun when it disappeared each night. A young man embarked on a mission to discover this mystery. He left his family, community, and country to dedicate his life to tracking the Sun's path. Many years passed — the young men became old, and the old men died — but the people continued to speak of him. Finally, he returned as an old man with the answer he had sought. He had journeyed to the great ocean and discovered that the sun sets and rises from the water. After sharing this profound knowledge with his people, the old man died, his life's purpose fulfilled. The story represents the Choctaw value of dedication to knowledge and truth.
source: Wikipedia - Choctaw mythology; Mould, Choctaw Tales

source_references.title: Handbook of the Indians North of Mexico
source_references.author: John Wesley Powell (ed.)
source_references.publisher: Bureau of American Ethnology, Bulletin 30, Part 2

title: Hoklonote: The Shape-Shifting Spirit (Choctaw)
summary: Hoklonote is a malevolent Choctaw spirit who can assume any shape it desires. This bad spirit is believed to be able to read people's thoughts, making it nearly impossible to hide from. Hoklonote takes different forms to deceive and harm humans — appearing as a loved one, an animal, or even an inanimate object. The spirit's ability to change shape and read minds made it one of the most feared beings in Choctaw mythology. Shamans performed protective rituals to ward off Hoklonote, and people were taught to be suspicious of unexpected occurrences or unusual animal behavior. The story teaches that evil can take many disguises and that spiritual awareness is necessary for protection.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Southeastern Indians
source_references.author: John R. Swanton

title: How Rabbit Brought Fire to the People (Creek)
summary: In this Muscogee Creek legend, the animals possessed fire but kept it hidden from humans. Rabbit, the trickster figure Chufi, decided to steal fire for the people. Rabbit crept to where the animals were guarding the fire and tricked them. He challenged them to a game and, while they were distracted, grabbed a burning ember. The animals chased Rabbit, but he was too quick and clever, passing the fire to the humans. This is why Rabbit is honored by the Creek people. The story differs from the Cherokee fire-bringing myth (which features Water Spider) but shares the theme of a small, clever creature overcoming larger, more powerful beings to bring the gift of fire to humanity. Rabbit's trickster nature is celebrated as ultimately benefitting the people.

title: The Hamatsa Cannibal Spirit (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Baxbaxwalanuksiwe' (Man-Eater at the North End of the World) is the most important supernatural being in Kwakwaka'wakw winter ceremonies. This cannibal spirit has mouths all over his body and can transform into various man-eating birds. His attendants include the Hamatsa bird-masks: Gwaxwgwakwalanuksiwe' (a man-eating raven), Galuxwadzuwus (Crooked-Beak of Heaven, a bird with a long, crooked beak), and Huxhukw (a supernatural crane-like bird who cracks skulls to suck out brains). The Hamatsa ceremony, the most prestigious of the T'seka (Red Cedar Bark Ceremonies), involves a four-day complex dance where initiates become possessed by Baxbaxwalanuksiwe'. The initiate goes into the woods for months, then returns to the village in a wild cannibalistic frenzy, calling 'Hap! Hap! (Eat! Eat!)'. He is gradually tamed by healers and society members through songs, dances, and rituals, finally emerging as a calm, respected member of Hamatsa society. The ceremony enacts the binding of cannibal forces for human benefit.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; U'mista Cultural Centre; Franz Boas

source_references.author: Franz Boas
