title: How Brer Rabbit Lost His Fine Bushy Tail
summary: In this pourquoi tale explaining why rabbits have short tails, Brer Rabbit once had a long, beautiful, bushy tail that he showed off constantly. Brer Fox grew tired of Brer Rabbit's vanity and devised a plan. During a cold winter, Brer Fox went ice fishing and called out to Brer Rabbit, showing him a string of fish he had caught. Brer Rabbit, greedy for the fish, asked how to catch them himself. Brer Fox told him to cut a hole in the ice, dip his beautiful tail in, and sit there until the fish bit—then pull up the tail with a mighty jerk. Brer Rabbit sat all day until his tail froze solid in the ice. When evening came, he gave a mighty jerk and his tail snapped off clean, leaving only the short stub rabbits have to this day. Brer Fox laughed heartily at the vanity that cost Brer Rabbit his finest feature.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.publisher: D. Appleton and Company, New York

title: High John the Conqueror and the Devil's Daughter
summary: High John the Conqueror, an African prince sold into slavery who became a legendary trickster, falls in love with the Devil's beautiful daughter. The Devil, enraged, sets John a series of impossible tasks to keep them apart: John must clear sixty acres of forest in half a day, plow the land, plant it with corn, and harvest it all before sunset. The Devil's daughter, who loves John truly, secretly gives him a magical axe that fells trees on its own and a magical plow that turns the soil without effort. John completes every task with time to spare. The Devil then tries to kill John by setting his bed on fire, but John escapes by shape-shifting. John and the Devil's daughter steal the Devil's swiftest horses and flee. The Devil pursues them, but John and his lover transform themselves into various forms—a pond and a duck, a church and a preacher—evading capture at every turn until they escape forever.
source: Zora Neale Hurston, 'High John de Conquer' (The Sanctified Church, 1943); snip Virginia Hamilton, The People Could Fly

source_references.title: Negro Folktales in Michigan
source_references.author: Richard A. Waterman
source_references.publisher: Journal of American Folklore, Vol. 69, No. 274

title: High John the Conqueror Brings Freedom
summary: According to the lore collected by Zora Neale Hurston, High John the Conqueror was more than a trickster—he was a spirit of liberation who moved invisibly among the enslaved. Born an African prince, John was captured and sold into slavery, but his spirit could never be broken. When the whip fell, John would 'take the spirit of his people out of their bodies' so they could not feel the pain. He could be heard laughing in the slave quarters at night, a sound that reminded the enslaved that freedom was coming. John planted the idea of emancipation in the minds of white people and provoked the Civil War itself. After emancipation, John returned to Africa, but according to Hurston, 'He left his power here' and 'he waits to return when his people call him.' High John's root—a dried tuber carried in a mojo bag—remains a central talisman in Hoodoo practice, symbolizing unbreakable spirit and the certainty of victory over oppression.
source: Zora Neale Hurston, 'High John de Conquer' (American Mercury, 1943)

source_references.title: The Book of Negro Folklore
source_references.author: Langston Hughes and Arna Bontemps (eds.)
source_references.publisher: Dodd, Mead and Company, New York

title: How the Sky-God's Stories Became Anansi's (Ashanti/Akan)
summary: Anansi the Spider wanted to buy the stories of Nyame, the Sky God, who owned all the stories in the world. Nyame set a nearly impossible price: Anansi must bring him Onini the python, Mmoboro the hornets, Osebo the leopard, and Mmoatia the invisible dwarf. Through clever trickery, Anansi succeeded. He caught the python by pretending to argue about its length and getting the snake to stretch out on a branch, then tying it. He tricked the hornets into a gourd by convincing them rain was coming. He trapped the leopard in a pit by digging a hole and covering it. He captured the dwarf using a sticky doll. Nyame was so impressed that he declared all stories would henceforth be called Anansi stories. The Akan word 'Anansesem' (spider tales) refers to this collection of stories that Anansi won for humanity.
source: Ashanti oral tradition, R.S. Rattray collection
full_text:
In the age when Nyame, the Sky God, still held dominion over all things, every story in the world belonged to him. The tales of heroes and villains, of love and loss, of creation and destruction—all of them rested in Nyame's palace in the sky, guarded by servants and locked behind doors that only the Sky God could open. The people of earth had no stories of their own. Their evenings were silent, their children slept without tales, and the elders had nothing to pass down. Anansi the Spider, who was already known for his cleverness and his appetite for trouble, decided that this was an injustice he could not tolerate. He climbed to the sky and approached Nyame with an offer: "I wish to buy your stories. Name your price."

Nyame smiled, for he had hoped no mortal would be foolish enough to try. He set four tasks that he believed were impossible: bring him Onini the python, Mmoboro the hornets, Osebo the leopard, and Mmoatia the invisible dwarf. Anansi descended to earth and set to work. He persuaded Onini the python to lie alongside a bamboo pole to prove his length, then tied him to the pole before the python realized what had happened. He waited for rain, then poured palm oil over the hornets' nest and offered them shelter in a gourd, trapping them inside. He dug a pit, covered it with a cloth and sticky sap, and waited for Osebo the leopard to fall in and become mired. And he left a trail of honey-covered dolls in the forest that the invisible dwarf could not resist touching, giving away his location. Anansi returned to Nyame with all four prisoners. The Sky God was stunned. He had no choice but to honor the bargain, and from that day, all the stories in the world belonged to Anansi. The Ashanti griots say this is why the spider tells stories to this day, and why every tale ever told begins with the debt that Nyame owed to a very clever spider.

title: How Death Came to Humanity (Yoruba/Benin)
summary: In the beginning, humans did not experience death. When they grew old, the god Olorun would rejuvenate them. A man named Iku (Death) was sent to test humanity. Iku appeared as an old man begging for food, but only one woman, a poor widow, shared her meal with him. Iku rewarded her by warning her that a plague would come and only those who stayed indoors would survive. When the plague struck, the disobedient died, and from that moment on, death entered the world. Iku was appointed as the one who carries souls to the afterlife. The Yoruba believe that death is not an end but a transition to the ancestral realm (Ọrun), where the dead join the ancestors and continue to influence the world of the living. This story explains the origin of mortality and the importance of hospitality in Yoruba culture.

source_references.title: Ifa: An Exposition of the Literary Corpus
source_references.author: Abimbola, Wande

full_text:
In the earliest days of the world, the Yoruba say, human beings did not die. When their hair turned white and their skin grew thin, the god Olorun would reach down from the sky and restore them. Their wrinkles smoothed. Their strength returned. They grew young again and lived another lifetime. Death, as we know it, did not exist. The world was a place of endless return, and the people knew no grief. But Olorun was troubled. The earth was growing crowded. Generation after generation accumulated upon the soil, and there was no room for new souls to be born. He decided to send a messenger to the world with two messages. One was of peace: "Tell the people that when they grow old, they shall die and return to me." The other was of renewal: "Tell the people that when they grow old, they shall be reborn and return to the world young again."

The messenger was Iku, a being of terrible aspect, gaunt and silent, with eyes like empty wells. He was sent to carry both messages, but Iku chose one—and one only. He appeared before an old man sitting outside his hut and said, "When you grow old, you will die, and you will not return." The old man wept. He called his family, and they wept too. Then a poor widow, the kindest woman in the village, saw Iku standing in the road and offered him food and water. Iku, moved by her generosity, told her the second message: "When death comes to you, you will return young to the world." The widow lived her life with peace in her heart, and when death came, she was reborn. But the rest of humanity received only the first message. From that day, death has walked the earth—a guest who was invited by Olorun, delivered by Iku, and accepted by a poor widow who understood that kindness is the only door through which eternity can enter.

title: The Heavenly Camel: A Somali Creation Myth
summary: In the beginning, the sky was low enough to touch, and the Somali people lived in close communion with the stars. The creator god Waaq formed the first camel from the morning mist and gave it to the people as the most valuable of all animals — able to travel for days without water, carry heavy loads, and provide milk, meat, and shelter. The camel was so beloved that the people became distracted by its abundance and forgot to pray. Waaq grew angry and lifted the sky high out of reach, separating humanity from the divine realm. But he left the camel as a reminder of his generosity. To this day, the camel is central to Somali pastoral life, and the best camels are said to be those descended directly from Waaq's original creation. Somali poetry and song celebrate the camel's beauty, endurance, and loyalty, calling it the 'ship of the desert' and the foundation of Somali wealth and culture.
source: Somali oral tradition, pre-Islamic Somali mythology
moral: Divine gifts must be received with gratitude, not taken for granted.

source_references.title: A Pastoral Democracy: A Study of Pastoralism Among the Northern Somali
source_references.author: Lewis, I.M.

full_text:
The Somali elders speak of a time before the Horn of Africa took its shape, when the land was flat and featureless and the sky hung low over the earth like a curtain of grey wool. In those days, Enkai—or Waaq, as the Somali call the sky god—decided to send down the first camel from heaven, for without the camel, the people of the desert could not survive. The heavenly camel was a creature of extraordinary beauty, its coat the color of moonlight, its eyes deep as wells, its hump full of the milk of creation. Waaq placed upon the camel's back the gifts that would sustain humanity: milk for nourishment, wool for warmth, dung for fire, and the patience to endure any hardship.

The camel descended from the sky on a rope of woven cloud, and when its hooves touched the red earth of the Somali coast, the ground trembled and cracked. From those cracks, rivers flowed—the Shabelle and the Jubba, the seasonal waterways that would sustain life across the Horn for millennia. The people gathered around the camel in wonder, and Waaq spoke from the sky: "Treat this creature with respect, and it will sustain you through drought and famine. Abuse it, and the land will turn against you." The Somali kept the covenant. They became the keepers of the camel, and the camel became the center of their civilization—its milk the foundation of every meal, its wool the fabric of every tent, its endurance the model for every life lived in the desert. To this day, when a Somali elder is asked about the origins of his people, he answers without hesitation: "We are the children of the heavenly camel, and the camel remembers the sky."

title: Hlakanyana: The Zulu-Xhosa Trickster Born from the Womb
summary: Hlakanyana (Uhlakanyana) is a cunning trickster figure in Zulu and Xhosa folklore, of weasel-like size and nature, who speaks from his mother's womb demanding to be born because the cattle of his father are being devoured. After birth, he embarks on a series of adventures: he tricks a cannibal by cooking the cannibal's mother and feeding her to him, escapes by turning into a stick, steals birds from traps, and outwits a hyena and a baboon. His stories emphasize the triumph of intelligence over brute strength and the importance of cunning for survival in a precarious world.
full_text:
In the beginning, before the world had grown old and weary, there lived a being so clever, so shameless, and so dangerous that even the gods trembled when his name was whispered. His name was Hlakanyana, and he was born from the womb of a woman who had been struck by lightning during a storm so fierce that the hills themselves shook. The child came into the world already laughing, his eyes bright with cunning, and from the moment he drew his first breath, nothing in creation was safe from his schemes.

Hlakanyana grew at an unnatural rate. Within days he was walking, within weeks he was speaking, and within months he had already outwitted every elder in his village. He stole milk from cows without touching them, tricked hyenas into giving up their meat, and once convinced a flock of birds that the sky was falling so they would fly low enough for him to catch them. The people of the village tried to keep him in check, but every punishment they devised, he turned to his advantage. When they tied him to a tree, he persuaded the ants to chew through the rope. When they locked him in a hut, he talked the termites into eating through the walls.

The elders consulted the sangoma, who shook her head and said, "This one was not meant to live among men. He belongs to the shadows between worlds." But no one could agree on how to dispose of him, and while they argued, Hlakanyana slipped away into the bush, where he roamed freely, stealing, tricking, and deceiving all who crossed his path.

His most famous exploit involved the cattle of the great chief. Hlakanyana approached the chief's kraal at dusk, disguised as an old woman carrying a calabash of beer. He offered the herdsmen a drink, and while they slept, he drove the entire herd away into the mountains. By morning, the chief's wealth had vanished, and Hlakanyana was feasting on the fattest ox by a secret fire. When the chief's warriors tracked him down, Hlakanyana simply vanished into a hole in the ground, cackling as they dug furiously around him.

But Hlakanyana's greatest trick was reserved for himself. One evening, as the sun bled red across the veld, he found a pool of still water reflecting the sky. He stared at his own reflection and saw, for the first time, the creature he had become — a thing of hunger and cunning, with no friend, no family, and no love. The trickster paused, and in that moment of stillness, something shifted in his hollow chest. But the moment passed, and Hlakanyana laughed, and the sound carried across the empty plains like wind through dry grass.

To this day, the Zulu and Xhosa tell stories of Hlakanyana to teach children that cleverness without kindness is a curse, and that the trickster who outwits the world will ultimately find himself alone in it. The tales of Hlakanyana remain among the richest trickster traditions in southern Africa, a reminder that the line between hero and villain is drawn in sand, not stone.

title: Heitsi-Eibib Defeats the Ga-Gorib
summary: Heitsi-Eibib, the ancestral hero and trickster of the Khoikhoi people, confronted the fearsome monster Ga-Gorib who sat at the edge of a deep hole, challenging passersby to throw a stone at his forehead. The stone would bounce back and kill the thrower. Unlike his predecessors, Heitsi-Eibib refused the challenge and instead distracted Ga-Gorib long enough to strike him behind the ear. Ga-Gorib lost his balance and fell into his own pit, dying in the hole he had prepared for others. Heitsi-Eibib is a death-rebirth figure whose funeral cairns are scattered across southern Africa, where travelers throw stones for good luck.

source_references.title: The Bantu-Speaking Peoples of Southern Africa
source_references.author: Hammond-Tooke, W.D. (ed.)

full_text:
In the deep antiquity of the Khoikhoi people, when the world was younger and the spirits walked openly among the living, there arose a monster called Ga-Gorib. He sat at the edge of a vast, dark hole in the earth, and his voice carried across the veldt like thunder rolling over the mountains. Ga-Gorib was cunning, for he did not hunt his prey with claws or teeth. Instead, he challenged every passerby to throw a stone at his great, bulging forehead. "Strike me!" he bellowed. "If you can wound me, I will yield my treasures. But if you fail, the stone shall return to you and end your life." Many had tried. Warriors, hunters, and even the cleverest of the Khoikhoi had accepted Ga-Gorib's challenge, only to see their stone bounce back from his hard skull and strike them dead. The ground around the hole was littered with the bones of the fallen, and Ga-Gorib grew fat on their misfortune.

Then came Heitsi-Eibib, the ancestral hero whose name is spoken with reverence across the dry lands of southern Africa. Heitsi-Eibib was no ordinary man. He had been born from a woman who had eaten a magical herb, and from his birth he possessed the gifts of cunning, courage, and an uncanny knowledge of the spirit world. He had wandered far and wide, outwitting monsters and spirits, and his funeral cairns are scattered across the landscape to this day, where travelers toss stones for luck.

When Heitsi-Eibib arrived at the edge of Ga-Gorib's pit, the monster issued his usual challenge. But Heitsi-Eibib did not pick up a stone. Instead, he studied the creature, noting how the light caught the moisture in his eyes, how his weight shifted when he leaned forward, how his confidence was a mask for something deeper. Heitsi-Eibib began to talk — complimenting Ga-Gorib on his fearsome appearance, asking about his origins, marveling at his great strength. The monster, unused to flattery, grew puffed with pride. He leaned closer to hear the praise, his balance shifting over the dark abyss he had prepared for others.

In a single, fluid motion, Heitsi-Eibib struck Ga-Gorib behind the ear — the one soft place the monster had not thought to protect. Ga-Gorib staggered, his legs buckled, and with a roar that shook the earth, he toppled backward into his own pit, crashing down among the bones of all those he had destroyed. The ground swallowed him, and the hole filled with silence.

Heitsi-Eibib stood at the edge for a long moment, looking down into the darkness. Then he turned and walked away, leaving no stone, no monument, no boast. The Khoikhoi say that his funeral cairns mark the places where he rested between journeys, and that to this day, travelers who toss stones upon these mounds are honored with safe passage and good fortune.

title: The Hare Trickster of the Tswana
summary: In Tswana folklore, the hare (mmutla) is a classic trickster figure who uses intelligence to overcome physically stronger opponents. In the well-known tale of 'The Lion and the Hare,' the hare repeatedly outwits the lion through clever schemes and quick thinking, teaching that wit and strategy can defeat brute strength. These stories belong to a wider cycle of hare trickster tales found across Bantu-speaking southern Africa, where the hare serves the same role as Br'er Rabbit in African-American folklore. The Tswana hare embodies the values of intelligence, resourcefulness, and the importance of community survival through cunning, providing moral education for children in an entertaining form.
full_text:
In the vast savannas of Botswana, where the acacia trees cast long shadows across the red earth and the wind carries the scent of dry grass and distant rain, there lives a creature so small, so unassuming, and yet so clever that even the great predators of the plains respect his cunning. His name is Mmutla, the hare, and he is the most celebrated trickster in all of Tswana folklore.

The tales of Mmutla belong to an ancient cycle of trickster stories found across Bantu-speaking southern Africa, where the hare serves the same role that Br'er Rabbit later filled in African-American folklore. But Mmutla is older than those stories, rooted in the deep traditions of the Tswana people who have lived on these lands for generations beyond counting.

In the most famous of his tales, "The Lion and the Hare," the great beast of the savanna — Nkwatlha, the lion — grew tired of the endless competition for prey and declared himself king of all animals. His roar was terrible, his claws were sharp, and his hunger was insatiable. One by one, the animals of the bush submitted to his tyranny, offering him the choicest cuts of meat and the freshest water. All except Mmutla, who simply continued about his business, hopping through the grass with an air of calm that infuriated the lion.

Nkwatlha summoned Mmutla to his den. "You are the smallest and weakest of all," the lion snarled. "Why do you not bow before me as the others do?" Mmutla blinked his large, round eyes and said, "Great king, I would bow, but I am afraid that if I bend so low, I will never straighten again. Perhaps you could demonstrate for me." The lion, eager to show his superiority, bent low to the ground — and Mmutla leaped over his back and disappeared into the bush, laughing.

From that day forward, Mmutla became the champion of the small and the weak. His stories teach that intelligence and strategy can defeat brute strength, that the clever mind is mightier than the powerful fist. Mothers tell these stories to their children at night, and the children carry them into adulthood, where they serve as a reminder that survival belongs not to the strongest but to the wily and the resourceful.

title: Hatupatu and the Bird-Woman
summary: Hatupatu was a young Maori warrior who was captured by Kurangaituku, a fearsome bird-woman with sharp claws and a taste for human flesh. She kept him in her cave dwelling, but clever Hatupatu bided his time. When Kurangaituku relaxed her guard, he escaped, running across the rugged landscape of the central North Island. The bird-woman pursued relentlessly. Hatupatu called upon the gods for help and was saved by the hot springs and geysers of Rotorua, where the scalding waters repelled his pursuer. He hid in a narrow rock crevice that Kurangaituku could not reach, and she eventually gave up the chase.

title: Hinemoa and Tutanekai
summary: Hinemoa, a high-born maiden from the shores of Lake Rotorua, fell in love with Tutanekai, a younger son of a chief who lived on Mokoia Island. Her family disapproved of the match. Tutanekai would play his flute across the lake each night, and Hinemoa listened from the shore. One night, spurred by her love, Hinemoa tied gourds to her body for buoyancy and swam across the cold lake in darkness. She reached Mokoia Island and found her way to Tutanekai by following his music. The lovers were reunited, and her family eventually accepted their union. This story is one of the most celebrated Maori love legends.

source_references.title: Ancient Maori Myths, Legends and Tales
source_references.author: A.W. Reed

title: Hi'iaka's Journey to Bring Lohiau to Pele
summary: Pele fell in love with Lohiau, a chief of Kauai, during a spirit dream. She sent her beloved youngest sister Hi'iaka to retrieve him. Hi'iaka agreed on the condition that Pele protect her sacred lehua groves and friend Hopoe. On Kauai, Hi'iaka found Lohiau had died of heartbreak and spent thirty days chanting to revive him. On the journey back, Pele grew jealous of Hi'iaka's prolonged absence. In rage, Pele destroyed Hi'iaka's lehua groves and encased Hopoe in lava. Hi'iaka, seeing this through far-sight, defiantly embraced Lohiau at the crater's edge. Pele engulfed them in flames. Hi'iaka revived Lohiau again, and ultimately Pele allowed them to return to Kauai together.

source_references.title: Legends of Gods, Ghosts, and Goblins
source_references.author: Laura S. Green

source_references.title: Pele and Hi'iaka: A Myth from Hawai'i
source_references.author: Natalie M. Gilbert

title: Hikule'o, Tangaloa, and Maui Divide the Cosmos
summary: In Tongan creation myth, after the primordial god Taufulifonua fathered Hikule'o with his sister Havea Lolofonua, the three great powers divided the universe. Hikule'o received Pulotu, the underworld realm of spirits and the afterlife. Tangaloa took the vast sky and heavens, becoming ruler of the celestial domain. Maui received the underworld (the earth's foundation) and was tasked with fishing up the islands from the ocean. This tripartite division established the cosmic order—the sky above (Tangaloa's realm), the earth's surface (under Maui's influence), and the underworld of Pulotu (Hikule'o's domain where souls journey after death). The balance between these three realms governs Tongan spiritual beliefs.

title: Hiro the Trickster and the Magic Canoe
summary: Hiro, the Tahitian trickster god of thieves and the son of the god Ta'aroa, built a magical canoe that could sail faster than the wind. Using his cunning and his enchanted vessel, Hiro traveled across the Society Islands performing daring thefts and outsmarting chiefs and gods alike. He stole fish from the markets, food from the temples, and once even attempted to steal the island of Bora Bora itself. His exploits angered the other gods, who eventually unleashed a great storm to stop him. The story of Hiro's rebellion and eventual downfall teaches that even the cleverest trickster cannot escape divine justice, but his resourcefulness remains celebrated in Tahitian storytelling.

title: Hotu Matu'a Discovers Rapa Nui
summary: King Hotu Matu'a lived in the mythical land of Hiva, which was sinking beneath the ocean. A prophet named Hau Maka appeared in Hotu Matu'a's dream and told him to search for a new island called Rapa Nui. Seven explorers were sent ahead in a canoe, finding the uninhabited island. They returned to report its location. Hotu Matu'a then sailed with two large canoes carrying approximately a hundred people, including his wife, children, and essential plants and animals. When they landed at Anakena Beach, Hotu Matu'a established the first settlement and divided the island among his descendants. His nine sons became the ancestors of the Rapa Nui clans, and he inaugurated the moai carving tradition to honor his lineage.

source_references.title: The Rapanui (Easter Islanders): Prehistoric Culture in Polynesia
source_references.author: J.J. Brémond

source_references.title: Aku-Aku: The Secret of Easter Island
source_references.author: Thor Heyerdahl

title: How the Moai Walked
summary: According to Rapanui oral tradition, the great stone moai statues were carved at the Rano Raraku quarry and then walked to their final ahu (ceremonial platforms) across the island. The moai walked at night, powered by mana from the ancestors channeled through the kahuna (priests). Using a system of ropes and rocking motions, teams would chant and coordinate to make the massive statues 'walk' upright, step by step, sometimes for miles. The largest moai, Paro, walked over 10 kilometers before taking its position at Ahu Te Pito Kura. One legend describes how a moai stopped mid-journey when the villagers broke a sacred tapu, and it remains fallen to this day, frozen by the broken taboo.

source_references.title: The Easter Island Statue: Style and Technique in Their Chronological Sequence
source_references.author: John P. Jennings and Christian E.岛

title: Hina: The Woman in the Moon
summary: Hina (also called Sina or Ina across different Polynesian cultures) was a mortal woman who became the moon goddess. In the Maori version, Hina went to fetch water at night and cursed the moon when she stumbled in darkness. The moon descended, caught her, and lifted her into the sky. In Hawaiian tradition, Hina was the mother of Maui who escaped human troubles and found refuge in the moon. Cook Islands stories tell that the moon god Marama fell in love with Ina and carried her away. In Samoa, Hina makes tapa cloth in the moon, her form visible during full moon nights. Across Polynesia, Hina represents the divine feminine, the cycle of death and rebirth, and the celestial light that guides voyagers at night.

source_references.title: Legends of Maui: A Demigod of Polynesia
source_references.author: S. Percy Smith

title: Hatuibwari: The Dragon God of the Solomon Islands
summary: Hatuibwari is a powerful dragon-like being from the mythology of the Solomon Islands, particularly among the people of Guadalcanal. Described as a massive serpent with the features of a dragon, Hatuibwari is a creator and cultural hero who shaped the islands and taught humans essential skills. In some accounts, Hatuibwari is a dual-sex being who created the first humans from clay and gave them fire, tools, and knowledge of agriculture. The being is associated with both creation and destruction, capable of bringing rain and fertility but also earthquakes and storms when angered. Hatuibwari's serpentine nature connects it to the widespread Melanesian motif of powerful snake deities who play central roles in creation myths across the region.

source_references.title: The Melanesians: Studies in their Anthropology and Folk-Lore
source_references.author: R.R. Marett

title: The Hintha Bird Legend
summary: The Hintha bird — a mythical golden goose or Brahminy duck — is the sacred emblem of the Mon people and the symbol of Bago (Pegu) Region in Myanmar. According to Mon Buddhist legend, the Hintha bird was the Bodhisatta (a previous incarnation of the Buddha) in the form of a wise golden goose. In the Jataka tale of the Hintha Bird, a pair of Hintha birds lived on a golden lake in the Himalayas. The king of the birds taught the creatures of the lake important moral lessons about non-attachment and the dangers of pride. The Hintha tradition also tells the story of how the Mon people, guided by a pair of Hintha birds, found their homeland. Two birds — male and female — led the Mon king and his people across the sea to the shores of southern Burma, where they established the kingdom of Suvannabhumi (Golden Land). The two Hintha birds, forever together, became symbols of Mon identity, representing fidelity, prosperity, and the golden purity of the Buddhist faith. Their paired image adorns Mon state flags and Bago's official emblem.
moral: Wisdom and moral purity, like the golden Hintha, illuminate the path to a promised homeland.
source: Mon Buddhist tradition, Jataka tales, Bago region folklore

title: The Hmong Creation Myth: The Flood and the Siblings
summary: The Hmong creation myth centers on a great flood that destroyed the world and the surviving sibling pair who repopulated it. According to the oral tradition passed down through generations, a great argument between the Sky Father (Saub) and the Earth led to a devastating flood that covered the entire world. Only two siblings — usually named as a brother and sister, known by various names in different clans — survived by taking refuge inside a large wooden funeral drum or a hollow gourd that floated on the waters. After the flood receded, they found themselves alone at the peak of a mountain. The brother proposed marriage to continue the human race, but the sister refused, believing it was wrong. After consulting with the spirit world, they were told to roll two millstones down opposite sides of the mountain; if the stones met at the bottom, they were meant to marry. The stones rolled and met, and the siblings married. The sister gave birth to a strange gourd-like egg or mass, which they cut open, releasing all the different peoples of the earth — including the Hmong, Chinese, and Lao. This legend explains Hmong ethnic identity and their relationship to neighboring peoples, while establishing the sacred bond between siblings and the role of divine guidance in human affairs.
moral: Divine will can transcend human conventions when the survival of all humanity is at stake.
source: Hmong oral tradition, Hmong shamanic texts, oral epics

title: Heer Ranjha — The Tragic Lovers of Punjab
summary: Heer, the beautiful daughter of a wealthy Jatt family in Jhang, fell deeply in love with Ranjha, a youngest son who tended cattle and played the flute. They met secretly for years until Heer's jealous uncle Kaido exposed them. Heer's family forcibly married her to another man. The heartbroken Ranjha became a wandering Jogi (ascetic), piercing his ears and renouncing worldly life. Traveling across Punjab, he eventually found Heer again. Her parents finally agreed to their union, but on the wedding day, Heer's uncle poisoned her. Ranjha, seeing her die, took the same poison and died beside her. The lovers are buried together in Jhang, where their tomb remains a pilgrimage site. Waris Shah's 1766 poetic rendition elevated this folk tragedy into the quintessential Punjabi romance.
source: Heer by Waris Shah (1766), Punjabi oral tradition
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

source_references.title: Heer Ranjha: A Study of the Punjabi Classic
source_references.author: Shamim Hanfi (ed.)
source_references.note: Critical study of the Heer-Ranjha qissa tradition in Punjabi literature, analyzing Waris Shah's 1766 version and its roots in oral Sufi folk romance.

title: How Anansi Got His Stories
summary: Long ago, all stories in the forest belonged to Tiger, and were called Tiger Stories. Anansi, tired of being mocked as the weakest creature, demanded that stories be named after him. Tiger set an impossible task: capture Snake. After failing with a noose, a pit trap, and a cage, Anansi tricked Snake by challenging his length against bamboo. Anansi tied Snake's head and tail to the bamboo, proving that brains can overcome brawn. From that day, all stories became Anansi Stories. The phrase "Jack Mandora, mi nuh choose none" is traditionally said at the end to absolve the storyteller of Anansi's mischievous deeds.
source: Martha Warren Beckwith, Jamaica Anansi Stories (1924); Real Jamaica Vacations
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

source_references.title: The Spider's Web: Anansi Tales Retold
source_references.author: Michael Rosen (illus.)

full_text: Long ago, all stories in the forest belonged to Tiger, and were called Tiger Stories. Anansi, tired of being mocked as the weakest creature, demanded that stories be named after him. Tiger set an impossible task: capture Snake. After failing with a noose, a pit trap, and a cage, Anansi tricked Snake by challenging his length against bamboo. Anansi tied Snake's head and tail to the bamboo, proving that brains can overcome brawn. From that day, all stories became Anansi Stories. The phrase "Jack Mandora, mi nuh choose none" is traditionally said at the end to absolve the storyteller of Anansi's mischievous deeds.

title: Hatim al-Tai: The Legend of Generosity
summary: Hatim ibn Abdallah of the Tai tribe, a pre-Islamic Arab chief in 6th-century Yemen, became the enduring symbol of Arab generosity and chivalry. Countless legends recount his extreme hospitality: slaughtering his prized horse when no other food was available for guests, giving away his possessions until left with nothing, and offering his own life to an assassin sent by a jealous king rather than sending the man back empty-handed. Even after death, legend says he hosted travelers from beyond the grave. The name 'Hatim' remains synonymous with generosity across the Arab and Persian world, and his son Adi became a companion of the Prophet Muhammad.

source_references.title: Medieval Arab Historians and the Fall of Civilisation
source_references.author: Amikam Elad

source_references.title: The Encyclopaedia of Islam
source_references.author: C.E. Bosworth, E. van Donzel, W.P. Heinrichs, G. Lecomte

full_text:
There was a man in the old Arabia whose name became the very word for generosity, and that man was Hatim ibn Abdallah of the tribe of Tai, who lived in the sixth century in the highlands of what is now Yemen, and who gave away everything he had and then gave away more than he had, and never once regretted it.

The stories are many and they all follow the same pattern: someone arrives at Hatim's door in need, and Hatim gives. He gives food when his larder is empty, he gives clothes when he has nothing left but the wrap upon his shoulders, he gives his horse when a guest arrives on foot and the journey ahead is long. The most famous tale tells of a time when guests arrived at his camp and there was nothing to eat -- his flocks had been scattered, his stores were bare, his wife had nothing to prepare. Without hesitation, Hatim went out and slaughtered his finest horse, the stallion that had carried him through a hundred raids and whose speed was legendary across the desert. He roasted the meat himself and served it to his guests, and when they had eaten their fill he sat with them and poured coffee and told stories, as if he had not just destroyed the most valuable thing he owned.

But the tale that surpasses all others is this: a king, jealous of Hatim's reputation, sent an assassin to kill him. The assassin found Hatim alone in his camp and revealed his mission. Hatim listened, then said, 'You have come a long way and you have nothing to show for your journey. Take these goods and give them to your king as a gift, and tell him that Hatim sends them with his compliments.' The assassin took the goods and returned to the king, who was so ashamed of his petty jealousy that he sent the goods back and sued for peace.

Hatim died and was buried in the desert, and even after death the legends say he continued to give. Travelers who passed his tomb at night reported seeing a light within the stone and hearing the clatter of plates and the murmur of conversation, as if Hatim were hosting a feast for the spirits of the road. Whether this was true or merely the wish of people who wanted the world to be a kinder place than it is, no one can say. What is certain is that the name Hatim al-Tai has been spoken with reverence across the Arab and Persian world for fourteen centuries, and that in a culture which prizes hospitality above almost all other virtues, there is no higher compliment than to be called a Hatim.

title: Hushang Discovers Fire (Sadeh Feast)
summary: The second king of the Pishdadian dynasty in Persian myth, Hushang, grandson of the first man Gayomard, one day hurls his flint axe at a serpent blocking his path. He misses the snake but the axe strikes a rock, producing a spark. Curious, the king strikes two flints together and creates fire deliberately — the first controlled fire in human history. He proclaims it a divine gift, makes fire his altar, and establishes the Sadeh feast to celebrate the discovery. Hushang also teaches humanity metalworking (fashioning axes, saws, adzes), irrigation, agriculture, animal husbandry, and fur clothing, fundamentally transforming human civilization.

source_references.title: Shahnameh: The Persian Book of Kings
source_references.author: Dick Davis (trans.)

full_text:
Hushang was the grandson of Gayomard, the first man, and he walked the earth in the earliest days of the Pishdadian dynasty, when the world was young and raw and the creatures of the wild still remembered the time before the sky was separated from the ground. He was a king, but kingship in those days meant something different from what it would later become -- it meant responsibility, not privilege, and Hushang bore that responsibility with the seriousness of a man who knows that every action he takes will be remembered for ten thousand years.

One day, walking through the hills with his axe of flint strapped to his waist, Hushang came upon a serpent coiled in the path, its hood spread, its eyes bright with malice. He drew his axe and hurled it at the creature, but the throw went wide, and the axe struck a rock with such force that a spark flew from the impact -- a tiny, brilliant point of orange light that danced on the stone for a moment and then vanished.

Hushang stared at the place where the spark had been. He picked up his axe and struck the rock again, and another spark appeared, and this time he was ready. He gathered dry grass and struck the flints together, and the sparks caught, and a thin curl of smoke rose from the grass, and then a flame, and then a fire -- the first fire that a human being had ever made by his own will.

Hushang stood before the fire and felt its heat upon his face, and he understood that this was a gift from the gods, perhaps from Ahura Mazda himself, the lord of wisdom and light. He knelt and gave thanks, and then he showed his people how to make fire, how to tend it, how to carry it from place to place. He taught them to smelt copper and tin and make bronze, to forge axes and saws and adzes, to dig irrigation channels and direct the water to their fields, to herd cattle and shear sheep and wear the pelts of animals for warmth.

And in remembrance of that first spark, Hushang established the feast of Sadeh, which is celebrated on the hundredth day after the winter solstice, when the fire on the hilltop is lit and the people gather around it and sing and feast and remember that all of human civilization began with a single, accidental strike of flint upon stone.
